title: Best Picture Power Rankings and the Winners and Losers of Movies in 2024. Plus: ‘Superman’ Is Here.
format: Sean Fennessey opens solo on whether 2024 was a good year for movies and the first 'Superman' trailer, then Joanna Robinson joins to hand out the winners and losers of the movie year and re-rank the Best Picture field. Sean closes with an interview with 'Nickel Boys' director RaMell Ross.

segments.heading: Was 2024 a good year for movies?

films.title: Kraven the Hunter
films.note: J.C. Chandor's entry in Sony's dying Spider-Man universe. Sean couldn't feel Chandor's point of view in it at all and skipped a full review.

films.title: Mufasa: The Lion King
films.note: Barry Jenkins applying his deep-focus close-ups to digital lions. Sean found it fascinating to watch but another mystifying live-action Disney remake; he's seen 16 of the 21 and rates none of them good.

films.title: The Lord of the Rings: The War of the Rohirrim
films.note: The new animated Tolkien film, part of the week's IP slate Sean chose not to devote time to.

films.title: Inside Out 2
films.note: Part of the case that movies are back: it exploded at the box office.

films.title: Wicked
films.note: Soared, another data point for a healthier-than-feared box office year.

films.title: It Ends with Us
films.note: One of only three non-sequel/prequel titles in the top 20 domestic grossers, alongside 'The Wild Robot' and 'If.'

films.title: The Wild Robot
films.note: One of the year's rare original hits inside the top 20.

films.title: If
films.note: The third original in the domestic top 20.

films.title: Rebel Ridge
films.note: Cited as a rock-solid genre movie that streamers picked up: a Netflix release rather than a theatrical one.

films.title: Carry-On
films.note: Netflix genre programming filling the gap left by theaters.

films.title: It's What's Inside
films.note: Another Netflix pickup Sean celebrates but wishes had played in theaters.

segments.heading: The ‘Superman’ trailer
segments.note: Sean and Joanna on the first look at James Gunn's DC reboot

films.title: Superman
films.note: The first teaser. Sean is all in: liked the color grading, the John Williams theme, David Corenswet's un-muscle-bound build, Rachel Brosnahan as Lois. Joanna is more skeptical of Gunn's tonal fit but loved Nicholas Hoult's Lex Luthor and the bloodied-in-the-snow shot. Both read Gunn as secretly a sap.

segments.heading: The winners and losers of the 2024 movie year

films.title: Deadpool & Wolverine
films.note: Sean's biggest winner is Ryan Reynolds, who engineered a billion-dollar movie at a low point for superheroes. Basically a MADtv sketch, but it kept the seat warm for a whole wing of Hollywood.

films.title: Wicked
films.note: The power-couple pick: Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo, whose much-mocked press tour turned into a friendship story that sold the movie to the world.

films.title: The Substance
films.note: Comeback of the year, Demi Moore, who hadn't headlined a studio movie in nearly 20 years and committed fully to a very strange film. Also a studio-of-the-year data point: Universal backed it but pawned distribution to MUBI. Margaret Qualley got famous off it too.

films.title: The Bikeriders
films.note: Part of Sean's case for Universal as studio of the year, targeting a range of demographics.

films.title: Twisters
films.note: A Universal hit and, later, a legacy-sequel loser: a shadow remake of the 1996 original. Sean was a true believer but found Lee Isaac Chung uninterested in doing anything beat-for-beat new.

films.title: The Fall Guy
films.note: Joanna's favorite of the Universal slate, part of the studio's filmmaker- and audience-friendly year.

films.title: Dune: Part Two
films.note: The bookend, with 'A Complete Unknown,' that makes Timothée Chalamet the movie star of the year. Villeneuve's choice to center Zendaya's Chani was a major adaptation improvement.

films.title: A Complete Unknown
films.note: Chalamet as Bob Dylan, James Mangold directing: the other half of his movie-star-of-the-year case and a genuine Best Actor threat. Also anchors the year's musical-biopic run.

films.title: Wonka
films.note: Cited as proof Chalamet can even carry a kids' musical, part of a deliberately eclectic CV.

films.title: Bob Marley: One Love
films.note: A big early-2024 hit and part of the musician-biopic wave that made musicals the genre winner.

films.title: Better Man
films.note: Michael Gracey's Robbie Williams biopic, with Williams played by a CGI monkey. Sean liked it more than 'The Greatest Showman': half stupid and boring, half exhilarating; the Take That street-dance number rocks.

films.title: Emilia Pérez
films.note: Another 2024 musical, and a shortlist juggernaut Sean expects to land 11 Oscar nominations.

films.title: Mean Girls
films.note: The musical remake, part of the year's strong showing for musicals.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: Zendaya's minting as a movie star beyond Mary Jane: a non-franchise turn Sean thinks will stay in the conversation for years.

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: Part of Glen Powell's riser case: a huge streaming hit that put him in the Golden Globe conversation.

films.title: Anyone but You
films.note: Carried over from the prior year to fuel the Glen Powell box-office-star narrative.

films.title: Alien: Romulus
films.note: Cailee Spaeny's riser year alongside 'Civil War': a franchise film and an exciting filmmaker at once. Also lands as a legacy tweener with great sequences and baffling choices.

films.title: Civil War
films.note: The other half of Spaeny's rise, an Alex Garland film that made money.

films.title: Terrifier 3
films.note: Indie story of the year: Damien Leone's decade-in-the-making slasher opened at No. 1 and has studio horror shook looking for the next one. Sean prefers 'Terrifier 2.'

films.title: In a Violent Nature
films.note: An IFC/Shudder film whose theatrical run did far better than expected.

films.title: Late Night with the Devil
films.note: Another Shudder theatrical overperformer, minting David Dastmalchian as a leading man.

films.title: Longlegs
films.note: Neon marketed the hell out of it to drive huge interest in a movie most people were mixed on.

films.title: A Real Pain
films.note: Headline title in Joanna's more-impactful-than-usual Sundance year; Kieran Culkin is about to win an Oscar for it, though it didn't do the commercial business a decade ago it might have.

films.title: A Different Man
films.note: A Sundance title still hanging around the awards conversation.

films.title: Dìdi
films.note: Part of the standout Sundance slate.

films.title: Will & Harper
films.note: The Sundance documentary that landed on the doc shortlist.

films.title: I Saw the TV Glow
films.note: Burbling in some awards circles; Sean tags it as your-favorite-filmmaker's-favorite-filmmaker, the movie he expects young directors to cite in 10 years.

films.title: Kneecap
films.note: Among the Sundance titles that made the most of a wide-open release calendar.

films.title: Between the Temples
films.note: Another entry from the strong Sundance class.

films.title: Sugarcane
films.note: One of the Sundance documentaries carrying onto the Oscar doc shortlist.

films.title: Soundtrack to a Coup d'Etat
films.note: A Sundance-premiered documentary on the shortlist.

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: Joanna's alt comeback pick, Adrien Brody as a bona fide leading man: captivating in nearly all of it, his body arced like architecture. Youngest-ever Best Actor once, now up against Chalamet, who'd be the youngest if he wins.

films.title: Venom: The Last Dance
films.note: One of the year's superhero losers; Joanna calls it a narrative disaster even though she wants to love a Venom movie.

films.title: Madame Web
films.note: Among Sean's worst times at the movies this year, part of Sony's collapsing Spider-Man universe.

films.title: Morbius
films.note: Sean rates it perhaps worse than 'Kraven' and 'Madame Web' combined.

films.title: Argylle
films.note: Part of Apple's terrible movie year: one of the most baffling things Sean has encountered.

films.title: Fly Me to the Moon
films.note: Not embarrassing, just a perfectly fine movie with two real movie stars dumped in mid-July: a symptom of Apple's movie problem.

films.title: Wolfs
films.note: The Apple Clooney-Pitt movie that made Sean and the hosts wonder why talented people's time was being wasted.

films.title: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
films.note: Legacy-sequel loser: a big hit but a mediocre movie with a bad script, even with Tim Burton back on practical effects.

films.title: Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga
films.note: The exception in the legacy-sequel pile: both agree it's good (a 7/10 for Sean) but it wildly underperformed.

films.title: Bad Boys: Ride or Die
films.note: A debatable legacy sequel that nonetheless made good money.

films.title: Gladiator II
films.note: Disappointing creatively but chugging toward profitability over the holidays; the CGI 'abominations' get a mention under digital monkeys.

segments.heading: The biggest movies of 2024
segments.note: Joanna's Trial by Content Q-score top 10, best-first
segments.subject: 2024's biggest movies

films.title: Dune: Part Two
films.note: No. 1: box office plus cultural stickiness, the year's clearest event movie.

films.title: Inside Out 2
films.note: No. 2: one of the three biggest movies of the year.

films.title: The Wild Robot
films.note: No. 3.

films.title: Wicked
films.note: No. 4.

films.title: Deadpool & Wolverine
films.note: No. 5: Sean argues its seat-warming importance is under-weighted by the algorithm.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: No. 6: the kind of movie that stays alive in the conversation for years.

films.title: Longlegs
films.note: No. 7: helped by the box-office-to-budget multiplier.

films.title: The Substance
films.note: No. 8: a movie people felt they had to see on a screen.

films.title: Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga
films.note: No. 9.

segments.heading: Best Picture Power Rankings
segments.note: Sean and Joanna's updated top 10, best-first (Blitz and The Substance dropped off the prior list)
segments.subject: Best Picture 2024

films.title: Anora
films.note: No. 1: holding, until Wicked lands a screenplay nomination.

films.title: Conclave
films.note: No. 2: the consensus second-place, a ranked-ballot favorite even if not the streaming icon Sean expected.

films.title: Wicked
films.note: No. 3: Joanna's pick could win if it gets a screenplay nod; the Jon Chu directing question holds it back.

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: No. 4: divisive; nearly blanked at the Spirit Awards, but the great-man craft-and-scale story the Academy still rewards.

films.title: Emilia Pérez
films.note: No. 5: six times on the shortlist; Sean expects 11 nominations.

films.title: Sing Sing
films.note: No. 6: way up, with Clarence Maclin, Colman Domingo, and its screenplay all in the conversation.

films.title: Dune: Part Two
films.note: No. 7: a little shaky, with Villeneuve apparently missing Best Director.

films.title: A Complete Unknown
films.note: No. 8: a traditional Best Picture type, lifted by Chalamet's Best Actor surge.

films.title: A Real Pain
films.note: No. 9: still competing, if a little softer than before.

films.title: Nickel Boys
films.note: No. 10: the movie Amazon/MGM is pushing hardest and the pick Sean makes as a throw to his RaMell Ross interview.

segments.heading: RaMell Ross on ‘Nickel Boys’
segments.note: the 'Hale County' director on his first scripted feature

films.title: Nickel Boys
films.note: Ross's first scripted feature, adapting Colson Whitehead. Shot in what the set called 'sentient perspective' (the camera as an organ, not POV), using narrow lenses and shallow focus to mimic the attention of consciousness. Half of festival audiences said they'd never seen anything like it; the other half said the same and didn't get it. The Chickie Peep bar scene, a 15-20 minute oner with Craig Tate, is the film's centerpiece; it ends on an Ethiopian jazz record Ross couldn't shake.

films.title: Hale County This Morning, This Evening
films.note: Ross's acclaimed documentary and the proof of concept for his observational-logic camera; the team finished it in obscurity expecting it to sit on a library shelf, then it broke through at Sundance behind 'Cameraperson.'

films.title: The Tree of Life
films.note: Ross's lodestar. Dede Gardner producing it is why he took the Plan B meeting at all: he figured if she could make that, maybe she could protect 'Nickel Boys.'

films.title: Cameraperson
films.note: Kirsten Johnson's documentary, placed in Sundance's New Frontier the year before 'Hale County'; Ross traces the lineage of risk-taking programming that made room for his film.

films.title: La Jetée
films.note: Ross's last-great-thing pick: Chris Marker's 30-minute film he rewatches monthly, a clear influence on his form.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: One of the commercial classics Ross grew up on before he turned the camera on himself.

films.title: The Shawshank Redemption
films.note: The other hardcore classic Ross cites from before his self-interested, spiritual turn toward filmmaking.

films.title: Lady in the Lake
films.note: Named alongside 'Enter the Void' as the first-person 'action cam' approach Ross wants to distinguish 'Nickel Boys' from.

films.title: Enter the Void
films.note: Gaspar Noé's film, cited as POV/GoPro-style cinema, the opposite of the sentient perspective Ross was after.
